text: The surgeon initiates the laparoscopic hernia repair by making an incision in the left subcostal region, employing an optical viewing technique. Six 5mm ports are utilized for access into the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon identifies the cecum and terminal ileum in the right lower quadrant and traces the small bowel retrograde, revealing abnormal adhesions between loops of small bowel and the small bowel mesentery.
The surgeon reflects a loop of small bowel towards the right abdomen, revealing an abnormal fusion between the left mesocolon and the small bowel mesentery.
The surgeon uses a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques to separate the left mesocolon from the small bowel mesentery, carefully freeing the adhered structures.

After lysing the adhesions, the surgeon visualizes the fossa of Lanzert and the hernia sac containing loops of small bowel. The surgeon then reduces multiple loops of small bowel trapped within the hernia sac.

The surgeon visualizes the fossa of Lanzert, located left of the fourth portion of the duodenum and posterior to the left mesocolon, which contains the inferior mesenteric vein. After completely reducing the small bowel, the ligament of Treitz is identified by running the small bowel retrograde.

The surgeon begins the hernia repair by closing the hernia orifice in two layers. The first layer involves placing a purse string silk suture to approximate the small bowel mesentery, proximal jejunum, mesocolon, and transverse colon.
The surgeon applies a running silk suture to reinforce the approximation of the proximal jejunum, mesocolon, and transverse colon, completing the second layer of the hernia repair.
The surgeon completes the hernia repair procedure by applying fibrin sealant over the closed hernia orifice, providing additional reinforcement to the surgical site.
